metadata.name: bact-math-utils
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3)
metadata.summary: bact: math and non mathematical utils

functions.name: bact_math_utils.tune.working_point_change
documentation:
Use betatron function to predict tune change

Args:

: dk: the quadrupole strength change dk
  beta: the betatron function. assumed to be constant accross the magnet
  length: magnet length

$$
\Delta Q = \frac{1}{4 \pi} \int_{s_0}^{s_0 + l} \Delta k \beta(s) d(s)
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.tune.tune_change
documentation:
Use betatron function to predict tune change

Args:

: dk: quadrupole strength change (normalised to dipole strength)
  beta: betatron function at magnet
  length: magnet length
  f: frequency of the machine
  nb: number of bunches

Measured tune change is

$$
\Delta Q_m = \frac{\Delta T}{f_m \cdot n_b}
$$

with math:`\Delta T` the measured tune change, math:`f_m` the main RF frequency and $n_b$
the number of bunches

functions.name: bact_math_utils.tune.to_momentum
documentation:
Deduces momentum from frequency

Args:
    alpha: alpha compaction factor
    freq:  current master clock frequency
    ref_freq: reference frequency of the master clock

modules.name: bact_math_utils.stats
documentation: Simple stat utils not provided by numpy

functions.name: bact_math_utils.stats.mean_square_error
documentation:
means square error from difference

Args:

: dv: difference (user needs to calculate A - B)
  axis: to sum over. see {func}`numpy.mean` documentation

  > for its usage

Returns:

: mean square error

functions.name: bact_math_utils.stats.mean_absolute_error
documentation:
means absolute error from difference

Args:

: dv: difference (user needs to calculate A - B)
  axis: to sum over. see {func}`numpy.mean` documentation

  > for its usage

Returns:

modules.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit
documentation:
Fit an exponential to an decay

with a twist towards real world measurement data

functions.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit.estimate_tau_inv
documentation:
Estimate amplitude and tau_inv for an exponential function

Args:

: indep : the independent (typically t)
  dep : the dependent variable (typically Tau)

Returns:

: c, tau_inv

Fits a linear regression to the logarithmic of the dependent

Shallow wrapper of {func}`numpy.polyfit`

functions.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit.scaled_exp
documentation:
Calculate a scaled exponential

Well, used for the fit functions ...

$$
y(t) = c * \exp{(t/tau)}
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit.scaled_exp_df
documentation:
Derivatives:

$$
\frac{df}{d\tau}  = - \frac{c_{0} t}{\tau^{2}} e^{- \frac{t}{\tau}} \frac{df}{c_0}    =  e^{- \frac{t}{\tau}} \frac{df}{b}      =  1 \frac{df}{d\tau_{inv}} = - \tau_{inv} c_{0} e^{\tau_{inv}                            \left(t - t_{0}\right)
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit.fit_scaled_exp
documentation:
Fit a decay function to the measured current

Args:

: t : independent variable (typically time)
  y : dependent variable
  p0 (optional, default=None) : start parameters

Fits the function {func}`scaled_exp` to the data t, y

$$
y = c_0  e^{- \frac{t}{\tau}}
$$

using its derivatives.

If p0 is not given it uses {func}`estimate_tau_inv` to estimate p0

uses:
: - {func}`scipy.optimize.curve_fit` for fit routine
  - {func}`numpy.polfit` to estimate p0 using a linear fit

functions.name: bact_math_utils.exp_fit.fit_scaled_exp_offset
documentation:
Fit a decay function to the measured current

This routine corrects for an offset (e.g. a calibration factor).
Furthermore it substracts t0 from t if t0 is given.

Args:

: t : independent variable (typically time)
  y : dependent variable
  b : offset
  p0 (optional, default=None) : start parameters c0, tau, b. This can

  > contain the value b as last parameter.
  > Will complain if the parameter b is
  > defined too

  t0 : start point of decay

Fits the function {func}`scaled_exp` to the data t, y

$$
y = c_0  e^{- \frac{t - t_0}{\tau}} + b
$$

using its derivatives. See documentation of {func}`scaled_exp_df` for its
derivatives

If p0 is not given it uses {func}`estimate_tau_inv` to estimate p0.

Warning:

: If using an offset, the estimate will be less robust. In particular the
  rather provide a good guess for the starting parameters. The estimate
  based on the linear model tends to faill.

uses:
: - {func}`scipy.optimize.curve_fit` for fit routine
  - {func}`numpy.polfit` to estimate p0 using a linear fit

modules.name: bact_math_utils.linear_fit
documentation:
Helper functions for linear regression

Regression function is not given as typically one would use
{func}`scipy.linalg.lstsq`.

Similar functionality is provided by
{any}`numpy.polynomial.Polynomial`

functions.name: bact_math_utils.linear_fit.x_to_cov
documentation:
compute the covariance for a linear regression

The linear regression could be computed e.g. by {func}`scipy.linalg.lstsq`
Mainly given here, so that it does not need to be repeated.

Args:

: X: independent variables
  N: number of data points
  p: number of parameters (including the bias or constant part)

See e.g. [ElemStatLearn] chapter 3.2

functions.name: bact_math_utils.linear_fit.cov_to_std
documentation: Compute standard deviation from co variance matrix

functions.name: bact_math_utils.linear_fit.linear_fit_1d
documentation:
fit a line and estimate the accuracy of the parameters

Args:
    x: independent
    y: dependent

Fits slope and intercept to the data

Returns: (p, dp)

modules.name: bact_math_utils.life_time
documentation:
@author: Pierre Schnizer

Calculate Touschek and Gas life time applying noise on the beam

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.lifetime_function_orig
documentation:
Lifetime function code directly implemented

Warning:

: Do not use it! Use {func}`lifetime_function` instead

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time._calculate_touschek2
documentation:
Calculate the *square* touschek lifetime

$$
\tau_{Touschek}^2 = b^2 + (c u)^2
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.calculate_touschek
documentation:
Calculate the touschek lifetime

Just applies {func}`np.sqrt` to {func}`_calculate_touschek2`

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time._liftime_function_physics
documentation:
Calculate lifetime from gas and touschek life time

$$
\frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{\tau_{gas}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{Touschek}}
$$

reformulated to use scaled 1 / (1 + q)

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.lifetime_function
documentation:
Calculate the life time depending on

Args:

: u : the noise level with which the beam was angeregt
  tauG : gas life
  b : constant contribution to Touschek lifetime
  c : linear scale factor for contribution to Touschek lifetime

Returns:

: lifetime (float)

Evaluates the equation

$$
\frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{\tau_{gas}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{Touschek}}
$$

with

$$
\tau_{T} = \sqrt{b^2 + c u^2}
$$

Its derivatives are required for the fit function. These were calculated to

$$
\frac{df}{d \tau_{gas} } = \frac{f^2}{ \tau^2_{gas} }
\quad
\frac{df}{db} = b \frac{f^2}{\tau^3_{T}}
\quad
\frac{df}{dc} = c \frac{f^2}{\tau^3_{T}}
$$

with $\tau_{T}$ the Touschek life time

The derivatives are implemented in {func}`calculate_lifetime_parameters_df`

TODO:
: - Should this code go into ? bact/bact/core/eV/life_time.py
  - Please give a reference for the equations

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.lifetime_function_fdf
documentation:
Derivatives of the lifetime function

For details see {func}`calculate_lifetime_parameters` and
{func}`lifetime_function`

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.lifetime_function_df
documentation:
Derivatives of the lifrtime function

For details see {func}`calculate_lifetime_parameters` and
{func}`lifetime_function`

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.calculate_lifetime_parameters
documentation:
Calculate the Touschek and Gas lifetime from noise measurement and t
life time measurements

Uses {func}`scipy.optimize.curve_fit` to fit the
function {func}`lifetime_function` to the set noise level and measured
life time

Args:

: noise_level : the noise level that was used on the beam (unit? )
  life_time : the measured life time (i.e. the current decay?)
  start_parameters (optional, None) : the start parameters to use in the

  > function

Uses
: - {func}`scipy.optimize.curve_fit` for fit rotine
  - ```
    func

     for derivatives
    ```

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.beam_decay
documentation:
Calculate beam decay

Args:
    t      : time
    u      : noise excitation
    c0     : beam current at time t=0
    u      : the noise level with which the beam was excited
    tauG   : gas life
    b      : constant contribution to Touschek lifetime
    c      : linear scale factor for contribution to Touschek lifetime
    offset : offset of the current reading of the beam current monitor

Returns:
    beam current

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.beam_decay_fsdf
documentation:
Calculate beam decay current at its derivatives

Args:

: See argument description of {func}`beam_decay_df`

Returns:

: (fs , df)

fs has to be multiplied with c0 to obtain f

functions.name: bact_math_utils.life_time.beam_decay_df
documentation:
Calculate beam decay derivatives

Args:

: See argument description of {func}`beam_decay`

modules.name: bact_math_utils.misc
documentation:
Provide enumeration given that value changed receently or completely new

Todo:

: find a new home for these tools

The naming follows
{func}`more_itertools.unique_everseen`

> and

func:`more_itertools.unique_lastseen`

Contrary to these, these class work use a string (not a single letter)
as an unique entry.

functions.name: bact_math_utils.misc.enumerate_changed_value
documentation:
Emit a new number every time the value changes

Uses {class}`EnumerateUniqueJustSeen`.

Warning:

: see {class}`EnumerateUniqueJustSeen` when using float values.

functions.name: bact_math_utils.misc.enumerate_changed_value_tuple
documentation: Emit a new number every time one of the values in the sequence changes

functions.name: bact_math_utils.misc.enumerate_changed_value_pairs
documentation:
Emit a new number every time one of the values changes

Uses {class}`EnumerateUniqueJustSeen`.

Warning:

: see {class}`EnumerateUniqueJustSeen` when using float values.
  Does not check both sequences are of equal length

functions.name: bact_math_utils.misc.exhaust_and_add_if_required
documentation:
Make a iterator of the given sequence, use prefix for filling up

Warning:

: Unchecked function, use with care

Args:

: seq: a sequence
  prefix: prefix to use for remaining arguments if required

Similar and inspired to {func}`itertools.zip_longest`

Yields item by item from the sequence. If further items are
requested, the prefix is used to generate new items. It uses
internally a counter. This counter is converted to the string
and added to the prefix.

Useful to be used to zip two sequences but contary to
{func}`itertools.zip_longest` a constant value is not
sufficient. E.g. you need to add a label to each item in an
other sequence

classes.name: bact_math_utils.misc.EnumerateUniqueEverSeen
documentation:
Return unique id's for the item and the item

Inspired by {any}`more_itertools.unique_everseen`.

Stores the unique_items in {attr}`unique_items` as
{class}`collection.OrderedDict`

Follows {func}`enumerate` API.

Warning:

: Internally values are distinquished if {class}`collection.OrderedDict`
  sees them as different values. So if using float values
  it can be good to scale the values and cast them to integers
  before using this class.

classes.name: bact_math_utils.misc.EnumerateUniqueJustSeen
documentation:
Return unique id's for the item and the item

Inspired by more_itertools.unique_lastseen.

Follows {func}`enumerate` API

Warning:

: Internally it uses '==' comparisons. So if using float values
  it can be good to scale the values and cast them to integers
  before using this class.

classes.name: bact_math_utils.misc.CountSame
documentation:
Enumerate how long the same value is seen

Follows {func}`enumerate` API

Warning:

: Internally it uses '==' comparisons. So if using float values
  it can be good to scale the values and cast them to integers
  before using this class.

  > value None will be considered as never seen

modules.name: bact_math_utils.distorted_orbit
documentation:
Utilities for closed orbit calculation

Todo:
    Use consistent variables: tune in mu or phi?
    Tune: call it tune or working point?

functions.name: bact_math_utils.distorted_orbit.closed_orbit_kick_unscaled
documentation:
Calculated closed orbit kick (not scaled by beta)

Args:

: mu: phase propagation
  tune: working point
  mu_i: phase propagation of kicker

Returns: unscaled kick

$$
\cos{\left(
    \pi Q -
          \left|{\mu_i - \mu(s)}
         \right|
 \right)}
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.distorted_orbit.closed_orbit_kick
documentation:
Calculated closed orbit kick (scaled by beta and theta)

Args:

: mu: phase propagation
  tune: working point
  mu_i: phase propagation of kicker
  beta_i: beta at the kicker
  theta_i: kicker angle

Returns: kick scaled by sqrt of beta and angle

$$
\sqrt{\beta_i}  \vartheta_i \cdot closed\_orbit\_kick\_unscaled
$$

functions.name: bact_math_utils.distorted_orbit.closed_orbit_distortion
documentation:
Calculate orbit distortion created by one kicker

Args:

: beta: betatron function along the ring
  mu: phase propagation
  tune: working point
  mu_i: phase propagation of kicker
  beta_i: beta at the kicker
  theta_i: kicker angle

Returns:

: closed orbit distortion

Computes the orbit distortion along the ring using the approximation of a small kicker.
Todo:

> check the statement on Floquet coordinates

Please note:
: - tune: working point of the machine in Floquet coordinates
  - phase propagation $\mu, \mu_i$ are given in $2 \cdot \pi$
    Floquet coordinates

$$
\frac{\sqrt{\beta(s)}}{2 \sin{\left(\pi Q\right)}}\cdot closed\_orbit\_kick
$$

full equation

$$
\frac{\sqrt{\beta(s)}}{2 \sin{\left(\pi Q\right)}}
\cdot  \vartheta_i \sqrt{\beta_i}
\cos{\left(
    \pi Q -
          \left|\mu_i - \mu(s) \right|
 \right)}
$$
